title: COVID-19 Vaccines
content: The first COVID-19 vaccine to receive Emergency Use Authorization was the Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine (BNT162b2), authorized by the FDA on December 11, 2020. Moderna's vaccine (mRNA-1273) received EUA on December 18, 2020. Both use mRNA technology. The Johnson & Johnson/Janssen vaccine (Ad26.COV2.S) received EUA on February 27, 2021 and uses an adenovirus vector.

title: Human Blood Types
content: The ABO blood group system was discovered by Karl Landsteiner in 1901, for which he received the Nobel Prize in 1930. There are four main blood types: A, B, AB, and O. Type O-negative is the universal red cell donor, while AB-positive is the universal recipient. The Rh factor (positive/negative) creates 8 common blood types.

title: Insulin Discovery
content: Insulin was discovered in 1921 by Frederick Banting and Charles Best at the University of Toronto. They extracted insulin from a dog's pancreas and demonstrated it could lower blood sugar. Banting and J.J.R. Macleod received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1923. Banting shared his prize money with Best, and Macleod shared his with James Collip.

title: Antibiotics History
content: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928. The first commercially available antibiotic was Prontosil, a sulfonamide developed by Gerhard Domagk in 1935, for which he received the Nobel Prize in 1939. Streptomycin, the first antibiotic effective against tuberculosis, was discovered by Albert Schatz and Selman Waksman in 1943.

title: DNA Structure
content: The double helix structure of DNA was described by James Watson and Francis Crick in their 1953 paper published in Nature. Their model was based on X-ray diffraction data produced by Rosalind Franklin and Maurice Wilkins. Watson, Crick, and Wilkins received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1962. Franklin died in 1958 and was not included.

title: Smallpox Eradication
content: Smallpox was officially declared eradicated by the World Health Organization (WHO) on May 8, 1980, making it the first (and so far only) human disease to be eradicated through vaccination. Edward Jenner developed the first successful vaccine in 1796 using cowpox material. The last natural case occurred in Somalia in 1977.

title: Human Heart
content: The human heart beats approximately 100,000 times per day and pumps about 7,570 liters (2,000 gallons) of blood daily. The heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, and left ventricle. A normal resting heart rate for adults ranges from 60 to 100 beats per minute.

title: CRISPR-Cas9
content: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was developed as a genome editing tool by Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier, who published their key paper in 2012. They were awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2020. CRISPR stands for Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats. The technology allows precise editing of DNA sequences.

title: Organ Transplantation
content: The first successful human kidney transplant was performed by Joseph Murray in 1954 between identical twins in Boston. The first human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard on December 3, 1967 in Cape Town, South Africa. The patient, Louis Washkansky, survived 18 days. Murray received the Nobel Prize in 1990.

title: Vitamins
content: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy. Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency causes beriberi. Vitamin B3 (niacin) deficiency causes pellagra. Vitamin A deficiency is the leading cause of preventable blindness in children worldwide.
